texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A new experimental method to obtain internal pressure profiles in a hollow fiber membrane was demonstrated. The experimentally obtained internal pressure profiles were compared with the theoretically calculated ones based on Hagen-Poiseuille equations. The experimental and theoretical results agreed very well in clean water conditions only when accurate membrane permeabilities and effective internal diameters were available. New experimental methods to obtain the two parameters were demonstrated. The same experimental technique was also applied for the submerged hollow fiber membranes filtering activated sludge to find out how internal pressure profiles were changing with time. Based on the pressure profiles, evidences that indicated the local flux near membrane exit was lower than those in adjacent area were found. This observation contradicted to the filtration models based on critical flux concept. It was considered that the cake layer collapse near the membrane exit was the cause. Though there was some degree of delay in pressures detection, the method demonstrated in this study provided a great accuracy when pressure profiles did not change rapidly.
texts.text: A new experimental method to obtain internal pressure profiles in a hollow fiber membrane was demonstrated. The experimentally obtained internal pressure profiles were compared with the theoretically calculated ones based on Hagen-Poiseuille equations. The experimental and theoretical results agreed very well in clean water conditions only when accurate membrane permeabilities and effective internal diameters were available. New experimental methods to obtain the two parameters were demonstrated. The same experimental technique was also applied for the submerged hollow fiber membranes filtering activated sludge to find out how internal pressure profiles were changing with time. Based on the pressure profiles, evidences that indicated the local flux near membrane exit was lower than those in adjacent area were found. This observation contradicted to the filtration models based on critical flux concept. It was considered that the cake layer collapse near the membrane exit was the cause. Though there was some degree of delay in pressures detection, the method demonstrated in this study provided a great accuracy when pressure profiles did not change rapidly.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most important aspects of the hollow fiber membrane is the internal pressure drop caused by flowing medium in the fiber. Due to the internal pressure drop, trans-membrane pressure (TMP) decreases along the fiber and local fluxes are not uniform over the membrane. This unbalanced filtration causes a certain degree of inefficiencies in terms of energy consumption and overall membrane performance.
texts.text: One of the most important aspects of the hollow fiber membrane is the internal pressure drop caused by flowing medium in the fiber. Due to the internal pressure drop, trans-membrane pressure (TMP) decreases along the fiber and local fluxes are not uniform over the membrane. This unbalanced filtration causes a certain degree of inefficiencies in terms of energy consumption and overall membrane performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to optimize the hollow fiber membrane modules, various mathematical modelings have been performed over a few decades [1-8]. All of these approaches relied on Hagen-Poiseuille equations to predict internal pressure profiles in principle. Assuming the internal pressure profiles were correctly predicted, local fluxes, local membrane fouling behaviors, overall membrane efficiencies, etc . were calculated [1-3,6]. Though the final mathematical outcomes often matched rea-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present work is about a methodology to accurately measure internal pressure profiles of submerged hollow fiber membranes. The experimentally obtained pressure profiles were compared with mathematically obtained ones. Based on the experimental data, a true permeability and an effective internal diameter of a hollow fiber membrane were estimated to use for the simulation. The same method was also applied to monitor the real-time internal pressure profile of a hollow fiber membrane filtering activated sludge.
texts.text: The present work is about a methodology to accurately measure internal pressure profiles of submerged hollow fiber membranes. The experimentally obtained pressure profiles were compared with mathematically obtained ones. Based on the experimental data, a true permeability and an effective internal diameter of a hollow fiber membrane were estimated to use for the simulation. The same method was also applied to monitor the real-time internal pressure profile of a hollow fiber membrane filtering activated sludge.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a submerged hollow fiber membrane, liquid permeates through the membrane from outside to inside and flows to exit due to a negative pressure applied to the exit as shown in Fig. 1.

This equation can be rearranged to Eq. (1 ′ ):

Internal static pressure is not necessary to consider in this equation because it always matches with external static pressure as long as whole fiber is immersed in the water.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Experiment
texts.text: 3. Experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hollow fiber membranes were taken from a commercial pilot scale module (Z-10 model, GE Zenon, Canada) and cut into eight 250-mm pieces to setup the experimental device shown in Fig. 1. The fibers had a dual layer structure that consisted of an outer membrane layer and an inner support layer. The nominal pore size of the membrane was said to be 0.04 𝛍 m and internal and external diameters were known to be 0.9 and 1.9 mm, respectively.
texts.text: Hollow fiber membranes were taken from a commercial pilot scale module (Z-10 model, GE Zenon, Canada) and cut into eight 250-mm pieces to setup the experimental device shown in Fig. 1. The fibers had a dual layer structure that consisted of an outer membrane layer and an inner support layer. The nominal pore size of the membrane was said to be 0.04 𝛍 m and internal and external diameters were known to be 0.9 and 1.9 mm, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 2a, each piece of membranes was glued into a T-connector hole by 5 mm using epoxy resin. Attaching two membrane segments in both sides of the 20 mm long Tconnector, 10 mm internal spaces were formed through which permeate flows. The pressure drop in the 10 mm space was considered negligible comparing to that in membrane fibers because its internal diameter was 2.5 times bigger than that of the hollow fibers (2.3 mm vs. 0.9 mm). In fact, pressure drop in a pipe is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the pipe diameter when flow rate is constant. Consequently, the pressure drop in the T-connector should be 1/39 (=1/2.5 4 ) of the pressure drop in the same length membranes. Overall eight segments and nine nodes were formed to make a 2000 mm (=250 mm × 8 mm) fiber, where active membrane length was 1920 mm. In each node, transparent flexible PVC tubes (ID 1.6 mm/OD 3.2 mm) were connected to read a pressure in the node (Fig. 2a).
texts.text: As shown in Fig. 2a, each piece of membranes was glued into a T-connector hole by 5 mm using epoxy resin. Attaching two membrane segments in both sides of the 20 mm long Tconnector, 10 mm internal spaces were formed through which permeate flows. The pressure drop in the 10 mm space was considered negligible comparing to that in membrane fibers because its internal diameter was 2.5 times bigger than that of the hollow fibers (2.3 mm vs. 0.9 mm). In fact, pressure drop in a pipe is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the pipe diameter when flow rate is constant. Consequently, the pressure drop in the T-connector should be 1/39 (=1/2.5 4 ) of the pressure drop in the same length membranes. Overall eight segments and nine nodes were formed to make a 2000 mm (=250 mm × 8 mm) fiber, where active membrane length was 1920 mm. In each node, transparent flexible PVC tubes (ID 1.6 mm/OD 3.2 mm) were connected to read a pressure in the node (Fig. 2a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane module with eight segments was attached to a stainless steel pipe (6 mm OD/4 mm ID) to support the structure of the fiber. Then, it was sled into a clear PVC pipe (50 mm inner diameter with 2400 mm length) with the nine flexible PVC tubes attached to the nine nodes. When aeration was required, the stainless steel pipe supporting the membrane module was used as an aeration tube. The nine flexible PVC tubes, which indicated internal pressures in each node, were arranged as shown in Fig. 2b. One more tube (3.2 mm internal diameter) was placed in the left side of the nine tubes to read the water level in the column, where one side of the tube was immersed in the column and the other side was lined up with other nine tubes. The water level in the control tube was always subtracted from those of other tubes to obtain the pressure.
texts.text: The membrane module with eight segments was attached to a stainless steel pipe (6 mm OD/4 mm ID) to support the structure of the fiber. Then, it was sled into a clear PVC pipe (50 mm inner diameter with 2400 mm length) with the nine flexible PVC tubes attached to the nine nodes. When aeration was required, the stainless steel pipe supporting the membrane module was used as an aeration tube. The nine flexible PVC tubes, which indicated internal pressures in each node, were arranged as shown in Fig. 2b. One more tube (3.2 mm internal diameter) was placed in the left side of the nine tubes to read the water level in the column, where one side of the tube was immersed in the column and the other side was lined up with other nine tubes. The water level in the control tube was always subtracted from those of other tubes to obtain the pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Before experiment, air bubbles in the tubes and the membrane were removed. While all pressure indicating tubes except one were stopped with clips, deionized water was gently flowed into the permeate tube using a 60 ml syringe so that the water flew out with bubbles through the opened pressure indicating tube. This procedure was done for all pressure indicating tubes from bottom node to top node.
texts.text: Before experiment, air bubbles in the tubes and the membrane were removed. While all pressure indicating tubes except one were stopped with clips, deionized water was gently flowed into the permeate tube using a 60 ml syringe so that the water flew out with bubbles through the opened pressure indicating tube. This procedure was done for all pressure indicating tubes from bottom node to top node.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Before testing with clean water, the permeate line filled with water was placed in lower position than the water level in column to develop a static suction pressure (Fig. 2b). Due to a very small membrane area (0.0115 m 2 ) and a low flux (<30 l/(m 2 h) or LMH), water level in the column decreased very slowly (0.29 cm/min at 30 LMH). Fig. 3 is an example of a pressure reading, where water levels in the control tube (W) were always subtracted from water levels in pressure indicating tubes (#1-9). Pressure (Pa) was obtained by multiplying a standard gravity (9.81 m/s 2 ) to the water level (mm). Permeate from the membrane module was collected in a small beaker for 1-5 min to measure a flow rate. The flow rate was again divided by membrane surface area to get a flux.
texts.text: Before testing with clean water, the permeate line filled with water was placed in lower position than the water level in column to develop a static suction pressure (Fig. 2b). Due to a very small membrane area (0.0115 m 2 ) and a low flux (<30 l/(m 2 h) or LMH), water level in the column decreased very slowly (0.29 cm/min at 30 LMH). Fig. 3 is an example of a pressure reading, where water levels in the control tube (W) were always subtracted from water levels in pressure indicating tubes (#1-9). Pressure (Pa) was obtained by multiplying a standard gravity (9.81 m/s 2 ) to the water level (mm). Permeate from the membrane module was collected in a small beaker for 1-5 min to measure a flow rate. The flow rate was again divided by membrane surface area to get a flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane module was also tested with activated sludge. The sludge taken from a pilot MBR was diluted to make a MLSS of 3000mg/l. Aeration was performed at 300 ml/min through the stainless steel tube supporting the hollow fiber membrane. Due to a membrane fouling at high flux (30 LMH),

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Membrane permeability estimation
texts.text: 4.1. Membrane permeability estimation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To calculate pressure profiles, flux in each segment should be calculated to get flow rates at the segment. The flux can be calculated with Eq. (3) and this equation requires permeability ( k ). The permeability of flat sheet membrane can be estimated experimentally with a good accuracy, where pressure drop along the membrane is typically very small due to a big channel crosssection area. On the other hand, for hollow fiber membranes, a significant portion of the TMP is caused by internal pressure drop and the estimated permeability using the TMP includes a significant error. If we know the exact average internal pressure of the hollow fiber membrane rather than the TMP that only indicates the exit pressure, more accurate permeability can be calculated.
texts.text: To calculate pressure profiles, flux in each segment should be calculated to get flow rates at the segment. The flux can be calculated with Eq. (3) and this equation requires permeability ( k ). The permeability of flat sheet membrane can be estimated experimentally with a good accuracy, where pressure drop along the membrane is typically very small due to a big channel crosssection area. On the other hand, for hollow fiber membranes, a significant portion of the TMP is caused by internal pressure drop and the estimated permeability using the TMP includes a significant error. If we know the exact average internal pressure of the hollow fiber membrane rather than the TMP that only indicates the exit pressure, more accurate permeability can be calculated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To estimate the average internal pressure, pressure profiles should be obtained experimentally. As described in Section 3, permeate was obtained by placing the permeate tube at a lower position than the water level in membrane container (see Fig. 2b). Once water levels in all the nine pressure-indicating tubes are stabilized, the levels were read. Suction pressure in each node was calculated based on the methods demonstrated in Section 3.
texts.text: To estimate the average internal pressure, pressure profiles should be obtained experimentally. As described in Section 3, permeate was obtained by placing the permeate tube at a lower position than the water level in membrane container (see Fig. 2b). Once water levels in all the nine pressure-indicating tubes are stabilized, the levels were read. Suction pressure in each node was calculated based on the methods demonstrated in Section 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, the measured flux, which was essentially an average flux along the fiber, was divided by the average suction pressure ( ¯ P ) to obtain a true permeability. These procedures were done for various suction pressures.
texts.text: Finally, the measured flux, which was essentially an average flux along the fiber, was divided by the average suction pressure ( ¯ P ) to obtain a true permeability. These procedures were done for various suction pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 1 compares the permeabilities calculated with average suction pressures to those calculated with TMP. The permeability calculated with average suction pressures was very consistent regardless of the flux. On the contrary, the permeability calculated with TMP tended to increase with flux in general. More importantly, the TMP based permeability was only about a half of that with the average pressure. The average permeability based on average suction pressure was 1.66 × 10 -9 m/(s Pa) and this value was used for further studies. When the permeability based on TMP (8.73 × 10 -10 ) was used for calculation, the calculated results were far off from experimental results.
texts.text: Table 1 compares the permeabilities calculated with average suction pressures to those calculated with TMP. The permeability calculated with average suction pressures was very consistent regardless of the flux. On the contrary, the permeability calculated with TMP tended to increase with flux in general. More importantly, the TMP based permeability was only about a half of that with the average pressure. The average permeability based on average suction pressure was 1.66 × 10 -9 m/(s Pa) and this value was used for further studies. When the permeability based on TMP (8.73 × 10 -10 ) was used for calculation, the calculated results were far off from experimental results.

texts.text: Fig. 5. Comparisonofexperimentalandtheoretical results for clean water conditions. Experimental and theoretical results are indicated with dots and solid lines, respectively. Effective internal diameter of hollow fiber was assumed 0.84 mm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the internal diameter of hollow fiber can slightly decrease due to a membrane shrinkage and a hydration during the filtration, all the calculations were repeated for various internal diameters from the known diameter of 0.9 mm down to 0.8 mm with a step size of 0.01 mm to find out which internal diameter predicted the experimental results best.
texts.text: Since the internal diameter of hollow fiber can slightly decrease due to a membrane shrinkage and a hydration during the filtration, all the calculations were repeated for various internal diameters from the known diameter of 0.9 mm down to 0.8 mm with a step size of 0.01 mm to find out which internal diameter predicted the experimental results best.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It was found that the calculated results with a diameter of 0.9 mm was far off from the experimental data while that with 0.84 mm fitted very well for all fluxes (data not shown). Therefore, the effective internal diameter in the model equations was assumed 0.84 mm.
texts.text: It was found that the calculated results with a diameter of 0.9 mm was far off from the experimental data while that with 0.84 mm fitted very well for all fluxes (data not shown). Therefore, the effective internal diameter in the model equations was assumed 0.84 mm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the internal diameter decrease was caused by membrane shrinkages, external diameter also should decrease somewhat. However, the inaccuracy in the external diameter does not affect the simulation result at all due to the exact offset in permeability calculations. If over estimated external diameters were used to calculate the permeabilities in Table 1, all the permeabilities in the table should be underestimated. When the amount of permeate passing through a membrane segment is calculated, the underestimated permeabilities are multiplied with the overestimated external diameter, where errors exactly offset each other. Consequently, errors in external diameter affect only the permeability of membrane fiber.
texts.text: If the internal diameter decrease was caused by membrane shrinkages, external diameter also should decrease somewhat. However, the inaccuracy in the external diameter does not affect the simulation result at all due to the exact offset in permeability calculations. If over estimated external diameters were used to calculate the permeabilities in Table 1, all the permeabilities in the table should be underestimated. When the amount of permeate passing through a membrane segment is calculated, the underestimated permeabilities are multiplied with the overestimated external diameter, where errors exactly offset each other. Consequently, errors in external diameter affect only the permeability of membrane fiber.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: model based on Hagen-Poiseuille equation predicted the experimental results very well. However, if the two critical parameters, i.e . membrane permeability and membrane diameter, had not been estimated with the new experimental method demonstrated in this manuscript, the theoretical results could not match with experimental results. For instance, if the ordinary permeability calculated with a TMP was used and/or if the measured internal diameter under non-filtration conditions was used, the theory should hardly predict experiments.
texts.text: model based on Hagen-Poiseuille equation predicted the experimental results very well. However, if the two critical parameters, i.e . membrane permeability and membrane diameter, had not been estimated with the new experimental method demonstrated in this manuscript, the theoretical results could not match with experimental results. For instance, if the ordinary permeability calculated with a TMP was used and/or if the measured internal diameter under non-filtration conditions was used, the theory should hardly predict experiments.

texts.orig: 4.3. Monitoring membrane fouling
texts.text: 4.3. Monitoring membrane fouling

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimental methods demonstrated in this manuscript can be also used to obtain internal pressure profiles with time. The pressure profiles with time will provide better understandings about the mechanism of the hollow fiber membrane fouling.
texts.text: The experimental methods demonstrated in this manuscript can be also used to obtain internal pressure profiles with time. The pressure profiles with time will provide better understandings about the mechanism of the hollow fiber membrane fouling.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the membrane, pressure drop also increases. The increase of the pressure drop in one segment relative to the previous segment can be attributed to two factors, i.e . (1) additional permeate in the segment increases pressure drop and (2) the flow coming to the segment from the previous one did not fully pass the previous segment so that the same flow can reduce more internal pressure. If pressure drop in one segment was far higher than that in the previous segment, we can postulate the flux in this segment is very high, especially in the upstream part, and/or the flux in the downstream part of the previous segment is very high so that the incoming flow to this segment does not contribute much to the pressure drop in the previous segment. On the other hand, if the pressure drop in one segment was not much different from that in the previous segment, we can postulate the flux in this segment is low and the flux in the upstream side of the previous segment is high.
texts.text: the membrane, pressure drop also increases. The increase of the pressure drop in one segment relative to the previous segment can be attributed to two factors, i.e . (1) additional permeate in the segment increases pressure drop and (2) the flow coming to the segment from the previous one did not fully pass the previous segment so that the same flow can reduce more internal pressure. If pressure drop in one segment was far higher than that in the previous segment, we can postulate the flux in this segment is very high, especially in the upstream part, and/or the flux in the downstream part of the previous segment is very high so that the incoming flow to this segment does not contribute much to the pressure drop in the previous segment. On the other hand, if the pressure drop in one segment was not much different from that in the previous segment, we can postulate the flux in this segment is low and the flux in the upstream side of the previous segment is high.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen in Fig. 7, pressure drop in the segment#7 is very close to that in the segment#8, while pressure drop in the segment#7 is much higher than that in the previous segment. According to the logic in the above paragraph, this indicates that there is not much additional permeation in the segment#8. This also indicates that there might be a high flux in the upstream of segment#7. The downstream side of segment#6 also can also have high permeation flow, but this high flux area should be very small judging from the fact that the spaces among the lines below segment#6 are rather regularly spaced. Overall, the flux in the upstream of segment#7 seemed to have the highest flux in the fiber with a possible high flux in a small area in the downstream of segment#6.
texts.text: As can be seen in Fig. 7, pressure drop in the segment#7 is very close to that in the segment#8, while pressure drop in the segment#7 is much higher than that in the previous segment. According to the logic in the above paragraph, this indicates that there is not much additional permeation in the segment#8. This also indicates that there might be a high flux in the upstream of segment#7. The downstream side of segment#6 also can also have high permeation flow, but this high flux area should be very small judging from the fact that the spaces among the lines below segment#6 are rather regularly spaced. Overall, the flux in the upstream of segment#7 seemed to have the highest flux in the fiber with a possible high flux in a small area in the downstream of segment#6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This observation does not agree well with the hollow fiber membrane fouling model suggested by Chang et al. [1]. According to Chang et al., if the local flux near the fiber exit exceeds a critical flux, this local area is fouled until the flux decreases to the critical flux and the lost portion of the flux is compensated in adjacent areas. This concept is visualized in Fig. 8, where the flux in fiber exit should be always higher or equal to any other part of the membrane during the course of filtration.
texts.text: This observation does not agree well with the hollow fiber membrane fouling model suggested by Chang et al. [1]. According to Chang et al., if the local flux near the fiber exit exceeds a critical flux, this local area is fouled until the flux decreases to the critical flux and the lost portion of the flux is compensated in adjacent areas. This concept is visualized in Fig. 8, where the flux in fiber exit should be always higher or equal to any other part of the membrane during the course of filtration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thediscrepancybetweenthetheoretical reasoning and experimental results can be explained by cake layer collapse theory [9]. According to this theory, the cake layer formed on membrane surface is exposed to vertical forces squeezing the layer
texts.text: Thediscrepancybetweenthetheoretical reasoning and experimental results can be explained by cake layer collapse theory [9]. According to this theory, the cake layer formed on membrane surface is exposed to vertical forces squeezing the layer

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: S.-H. Yoon et al. / Journal of Membrane Science 310 (2008) 7-12
texts.text: S.-H. Yoon et al. / Journal of Membrane Science 310 (2008) 7-12

texts.label: text
texts.orig: due to water permeation. The higher the permeation rate (or flux) is, the bigger the force is. Once the cake layer is exposed to a force (or flux) higher than a critical level, the cake layer will collapse and the resistance surges.
texts.text: due to water permeation. The higher the permeation rate (or flux) is, the bigger the force is. Once the cake layer is exposed to a force (or flux) higher than a critical level, the cake layer will collapse and the resistance surges.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the initial flux in the segment#8 was higher than a critical cake-collapsing flux, the initial cake layer formed in the segment will collapse to make the local flux very low. Then the flux in the adjacent section (segment#7) should increase to compensate the lost flux. If the resulting flux in the segment#7 is lower than the cake-collapsing critical flux, the cake layer in this segment will remain intact and the high flux can sustain. The initial flux at the end of the fiber should be 54.89 LMH considering the initial pressure in node#9 (9199 Pa) and the permeability of the membrane (1.66 × 10 -9 m/(s Pa)). With a combination of a low aeration, i.e . 300 ml/min, this flux might be enough to trigger the cake collapse.
texts.text: If the initial flux in the segment#8 was higher than a critical cake-collapsing flux, the initial cake layer formed in the segment will collapse to make the local flux very low. Then the flux in the adjacent section (segment#7) should increase to compensate the lost flux. If the resulting flux in the segment#7 is lower than the cake-collapsing critical flux, the cake layer in this segment will remain intact and the high flux can sustain. The initial flux at the end of the fiber should be 54.89 LMH considering the initial pressure in node#9 (9199 Pa) and the permeability of the membrane (1.66 × 10 -9 m/(s Pa)). With a combination of a low aeration, i.e . 300 ml/min, this flux might be enough to trigger the cake collapse.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This observation implies that too high permeability of hollow fiber membrane may not help improve the performance of hollow fiber membranes. The high permeability induces more unequal filtration in the membrane and then the cake layer near the exit becomes more prone to collapse. Eventually, cake collapses propagates along the fiber until steady state establishes at certain point. Conceptually, the most desired permeability is the maximum permeability that does not allow any local flux higher than a critical cake-collapsing flux, while average flux is maintained at a design flux.
texts.text: This observation implies that too high permeability of hollow fiber membrane may not help improve the performance of hollow fiber membranes. The high permeability induces more unequal filtration in the membrane and then the cake layer near the exit becomes more prone to collapse. Eventually, cake collapses propagates along the fiber until steady state establishes at certain point. Conceptually, the most desired permeability is the maximum permeability that does not allow any local flux higher than a critical cake-collapsing flux, while average flux is maintained at a design flux.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Limitations of the experimental method
texts.text: 4.4. Limitations of the experimental method

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The methodology used in this experiment was very useful to measure pressure profiles inside a hollow fiber membrane with some limitations. Due to the filling time and the draining time in the pressure indicating tubes (Fig. 2b), the pressure in nodes could not be measured instantaneously.
texts.text: The methodology used in this experiment was very useful to measure pressure profiles inside a hollow fiber membrane with some limitations. Due to the filling time and the draining time in the pressure indicating tubes (Fig. 2b), the pressure in nodes could not be measured instantaneously.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For instance, when suction pressure is increasing, the water level in tube#9 should decrease. Due to the draining time of the water through a main permeate tube, however, it took a while to reach an equilibrium condition fully (up to 2 min). In addition, the narrow lumen of the pressure indicating tubes (1.6 mm) and their length (4-6 m) interfered water movement. If bigger tubes are used, interference from the pressure drop can be reduced, but draining time will increase instead. As a consequence, this method could not capture the instantaneous pressure especially when membrane fouling was occurring rapidly. Therefore, the initial pressures in each node in Fig. 6 were plotted with the data obtained with deionized water at the same flux.
texts.text: For instance, when suction pressure is increasing, the water level in tube#9 should decrease. Due to the draining time of the water through a main permeate tube, however, it took a while to reach an equilibrium condition fully (up to 2 min). In addition, the narrow lumen of the pressure indicating tubes (1.6 mm) and their length (4-6 m) interfered water movement. If bigger tubes are used, interference from the pressure drop can be reduced, but draining time will increase instead. As a consequence, this method could not capture the instantaneous pressure especially when membrane fouling was occurring rapidly. Therefore, the initial pressures in each node in Fig. 6 were plotted with the data obtained with deionized water at the same flux.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this present study, a new experimental method to measure pressure profiles in a hollow fiber membrane was demonstrated.
texts.text: In this present study, a new experimental method to measure pressure profiles in a hollow fiber membrane was demonstrated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimentally obtained pressure profiles were compared with theoretically calculated ones based on Hagen-Poiseuille equation for clean water conditions. This method was also used to monitor internal pressure profiles during the filtration of activated sludge. As results, following conclusions were drawn:
texts.text: The experimentally obtained pressure profiles were compared with theoretically calculated ones based on Hagen-Poiseuille equation for clean water conditions. This method was also used to monitor internal pressure profiles during the filtration of activated sludge. As results, following conclusions were drawn:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Experimentally obtained pressure profiles agreed very well with theoretically predicted ones in clean water conditions, where no membrane fouling occurred.
texts.text: Experimentally obtained pressure profiles agreed very well with theoretically predicted ones in clean water conditions, where no membrane fouling occurred.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. For an accurate prediction, model equations required accurate membrane permeabilities and effective internal membrane diameters. These two parameters could be obtained with the new experimental method.
texts.text: For an accurate prediction, model equations required accurate membrane permeabilities and effective internal membrane diameters. These two parameters could be obtained with the new experimental method.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. When hollow fiber membranes were used to filter activated sludge, the local flux near the membrane exit did not seem to be the highest in the whole membrane. This observation contradicted to the filtration model based on critical flux concept. The cake layer formed on the membrane surface near the membrane exit was considered to collapse due to a high initial flux.
texts.text: When hollow fiber membranes were used to filter activated sludge, the local flux near the membrane exit did not seem to be the highest in the whole membrane. This observation contradicted to the filtration model based on critical flux concept. The cake layer formed on the membrane surface near the membrane exit was considered to collapse due to a high initial flux.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. The method proposed in this study provided accurate internal pressure profiles of hollow fiber membrane especially when pressure profiles did not change rapidly.
texts.text: The method proposed in this study provided accurate internal pressure profiles of hollow fiber membrane especially when pressure profiles did not change rapidly.
